query_examples: Explain binary search algorithm with example
When should I use binary search instead of linear search?
Binary search time complexity and implementation
Why is binary search O(log n)?
title: Binary Search Algorithm
context_variants: Beginner: a student who just learned about arrays and wants to understand searching
Expert: a developer asking about edge cases and performance characteristics
Confused: user who can't understand why we divide by 2 each time
Time-constrained: someone in an interview needing a quick refresher
content:
Binary search is an efficient algorithm for finding a target value in a **sorted** array. Instead of checking every element one-by-one (linear search), it repeatedly halves the search space.

**How it works:**
1. Look at the middle element of the array.
2. If it equals the target — done.
3. If the target is smaller, search the left half.
4. If the target is larger, search the right half.
5. Repeat until found or the search space is empty.

**Why it's fast:** Each step eliminates half the remaining elements. A sorted array of 1,000,000 elements needs at most 20 comparisons. This gives it O(log n) time complexity.

**Key requirement:** The array must be sorted. Binary search fails on unsorted data.
step_by_step_explanation: Step 1: Set left = 0 (start of array), right = len(arr) - 1 (end of array)
Step 2: Compute mid = (left + right) // 2 — the middle index
Step 3: Compare arr[mid] with the target
Step 4a: If arr[mid] == target, return mid (found!)
Step 4b: If arr[mid] < target, set left = mid + 1 (search right half)
Step 4c: If arr[mid] > target, set right = mid - 1 (search left half)
Step 5: Repeat steps 2–4 until left > right (not found, return -1)
common_misconceptions: Binary search works on unsorted arrays — it does NOT; sorting is a prerequisite
mid = (left + right) / 2 is always safe — in languages with fixed-size integers, this can overflow; use mid = left + (right - left) // 2
Binary search always returns the first occurrence — it returns any occurrence; finding the leftmost requires a modified version
Binary search is always better than linear search — for very small arrays or unsorted data, linear search may be preferred
edge_cases: Empty array: left > right immediately, return -1
Single-element array: check if arr[0] == target
Duplicate elements: standard binary search returns an arbitrary match; use 'leftmost' variant if needed
Target smaller than all elements or larger than all elements
Integer overflow when computing mid in languages like Java/C++
real_world_examples: Searching a word in a sorted dictionary
Finding a player rank in a sorted leaderboard
Locating a record by ID in a sorted database index
Git bisect — binary searching through commits to find a bug
Searching in standard library functions like Python's bisect module
analogies: Binary search is like guessing a number from 1–100: ask 'is it higher or lower than 50?', then 'higher or lower than 75?', etc. — you find it in at most 7 guesses
It's like finding a word in a physical dictionary — you open to the middle, see if you're too early or too late, then flip accordingly
allowed_scope: Can explain the algorithm, provide implementations in Python/Java/C++, discuss time/space complexity, and cover edge cases. Cannot guarantee correctness of custom user-provided data structures.
summary: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space, achieving O(log n) time complexity. It requires the input to be sorted and uses left/right pointers converging on the midpoint.
